planting.note: Plant container stock in late winter, spring, or fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Sweet Olive is a signature shrub of Gulf Coast and Deep South gardens in zones 8–10, prized above nearly all other plants for its intensely sweet, apricot-like fragrance.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to part shade; Sweet Olive performs best with at least 4 hours of sun but tolerates and even thrives in the dappled shade of open woodland gardens, where its fragrance is often more concentrated. Osmanthus fragrans is perhaps the most intensely fragrant shrub or small tree in temperate horticulture — its tiny, creamy-white to pale-apricot flowers, only a few millimeters across and produced in small axillary clusters, carry an extraordinarily powerful and sweet fragrance described variously as apricot, peach, jasmine, and vanilla that can perfume an entire garden on still evenings, carrying 50–100 feet from the plant. The fragrance blooms emerge in fall (September–November), often again in spring, and sporadically year-round in warm climates — the fall flush, just as the garden is losing other interest, makes it especially prized. In zones 8–10 it grows as an evergreen shrub or small multi-stem tree 6–20 feet tall; in zone 7 it may be semi-evergreen and die back to the roots in severe winters. The form 'Aurantiacus' has orange flowers with even more intense fragrance than the typical white-flowered form. Essential for zone 9 gardens.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Ornamental Plants

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring, summer, or fall in moist to moderately moist, well-drained soil in full sun to full shade; Nandina is one of the most adaptable ornamental shrubs for the Southeast and Gulf Coast, tolerating clay soil, drought, dense shade, and coastal conditions better than almost any comparable plant.

directions: Plant in virtually any well-drained to moderately moist soil in full sun to full shade; Nandina is one of the toughest and most site-adaptable ornamentals for Southern gardens, though it develops the best red fall and winter foliage color in full sun, and in heavy shade may remain more green year-round. Nandina domestica (heavenly bamboo — not a true bamboo) is an elegant, upright evergreen shrub native to China and Japan with fine-textured, pinnate (bamboo-like) foliage that emerges bronze-red in spring, turns green in summer, and becomes brilliant red to burgundy-red in fall and winter, especially in sun; large, pyramidal clusters of white flowers in May–June are followed by persistent clusters of bright-red berries that last through winter and provide exceptional wildlife value. The berries are relished by Cedar Waxwings and American Robins. Standard Nandina grows 4–6 feet tall; compact cultivars like 'Harbour Dwarf' (2–3 feet), 'Fire Power' (2 feet, intense red fall color), and 'Lemon Lime' (lime-green foliage) are popular. Note: Nandina berries are toxic to dogs and cats; berries may be invasive in some southeastern states — check local regulations.

common_name: Oregon Grape Holly (Tall Oregon Grape, Mountain Grape)

planting.note: Plant container stock or bare-root plants in spring or fall in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in part shade to full shade; Oregon grape holly is native to Pacific Northwest forests and is most successful east of the Rockies in part shade with protection from winter winds and afternoon sun.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in part shade to full shade; avoid exposed sites with afternoon sun or harsh winter winds, which cause foliage burn; protect with burlap in zone 5. Mahonia aquifolium is a Pacific Northwest native evergreen shrub with bold, leathery, pinnate leaves composed of 5–9 spiny, holly-like leaflets that are lustrous dark-green in summer and turn attractive bronze-purple to red-purple in fall and winter, especially in sun — a highly ornamental combination with the spring flowers and summer berries. Dense clusters of brilliant-yellow flowers cover the branch tips in March–April before most other spring-flowering shrubs, providing exceptional early-season pollinator value; the flowers are followed by clusters of blue-black, grape-like berries coated in blue-white bloom (powder) that ripen in August and are relished by many birds. The berries are edible (tart, high in vitamin C) and can be used for jelly or syrup; the roots and inner bark yield a yellow dye historically used by Indigenous peoples. The genus Mahonia is now often merged with Berberis by modern taxonomists.

common_name: Japanese Pieris (Lily of the Valley Shrub, Andromeda)

planting.note: Plant container stock in late winter or spring in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, acidic (pH 4.5–6.0), fertile soil in part shade; Japanese pieris shares cultural requirements with rhododendrons and azaleas and thrives alongside them in woodland garden settings with dappled shade and acid soil.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, humus-rich, strongly acidic (pH 4.5–6.0) soil in part shade; mulch heavily to maintain soil moisture and acidity, and protect from harsh afternoon sun and winter winds in colder zones. Pieris japonica is an elegant, slow-growing evergreen shrub that earns three-season ornamental interest through brilliant red to bronze new growth emerging in spring (one of the most vivid of any evergreen shrub), cascading clusters of white to pink, urn-shaped flowers in February–April (often starting when snow is still on the ground), and long-persistent ornamental seed capsules that hang in drooping chains through winter. The flowers closely resemble lily-of-the-valley blooms at much larger scale. The glossy, dark-green, lance-shaped leaves provide handsome year-round structure. Cultivars range from compact ('Cavatine,' 2–3 feet; 'Valley Valentine,' red flowers) to large ('Forest Flame,' fire-red new growth); the species is somewhat deer-resistant and is fully evergreen in zones 5–8, making it outstanding for year-round structure in acidic-soil woodland gardens alongside azaleas and rhododendrons.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring, summer, or fall in moist to wet, well-drained to poorly drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Walter viburnum is one of the most versatile and tough native Florida and Gulf Coast shrubs, thriving in both wet and dry soils and performing well from full sun to part shade.

directions: Plant in moist to wet or well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to part shade; Walter viburnum tolerates flooding, drought, clay, and sand — one of the most site-adaptable native shrubs for Florida and the Gulf Coast. Viburnum obovatum is a native Florida and Gulf Coast viburnum with small, leathery, dark-green, semi-evergreen to evergreen leaves that give the plant a fine texture uncommon among viburnums; flat-topped, 2-to-3-inch clusters of small white flowers appear in February–March and are followed by red berries that ripen to black, providing outstanding value for songbirds including thrushes, mockingbirds, and waxwings through winter. The shrub ranges from 3–12 feet tall depending on conditions — compact in dry, sunny sites and larger in moist, shaded conditions — and responds well to pruning into a dense hedge or wildlife planting. The cultivar 'Mrs. Schiller's Delight' is a compact, 3–4 foot selection with proportionally small leaves, ideal for residential gardens. Walter viburnum is native to wet flatwoods, swamp margins, and stream banks from Virginia to Florida and is an outstanding choice for rain gardens, wet spots, and bird gardens in zones 7–10.
